content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To       : Commissioners Houston, Lapan,
           McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn

Date  : March 20, 1980


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Barbara Milman

Subject: The scope of the Commission's discretion in imposing penalties
         under Section 83117(c

     Section 83117 sets forth a range of penalties which the Commission
can impose on a person found to have violated the provisions
of the Political Reform Act. The Act does not establish any rules
or criteria for the Commission to use in imposing these penalties,
other than the general charge in Section 81004.5 that the Commission
be "effective" and "impartial" in its administration of the Act.=

     The Commission has, to some extent, defined its own criteria
in its regulations. In 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18361(e) the
Commission has listed the following factors which it will consider:

     (A)   The seriousness of the violation;

     (B)   The presence or absence of any intention to conceal,
deceive, or mislead;

     (C)   Whether the violation was deliberate, negligent, or
inadvertent;

     (D)   Whether the violator demonstrated good faith by consulting
the Commission staff or any other government agency; and

     (E)   Whether the violation was isolated or part of a pattern,
and whether the violator has a prior record of violations of the
Act.


_______________________________

     1/  Section 91001(c) which is found in Chapter 11 (Civil
Enforcement) of the Act requires the Commission when acting as a
civil prosecutor to consider whether the violation was inadvertent,
negligent, deliberate, and whether or not the person has acted
in good faith. These considerations impliedly should also guide
the Commission in its administrative hearings conducted under
Chapter 3.

Commissioners Houston, Lapan,
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn
March 20, 1980
Page Two


However, the regulations do not prevent the Commission from
considering other factors. On the contrary, the regulations re-
quire the Commission to consider "all the surrounding circumstances"
as well as the factors specifically mentioned.

The Act and the regulations, therefore, leave the Commission
very broad discretion in the imposition of penalties. In exercising
this discretion the Commission should keep in mind some guiding
principles developed by the courts for administrative agencies
with powers similar to those of the Commission.

As a general matter, courts are very reluctant to upset an
administrative agency's imposition of a penalty. Courts have said
over and over that they will uphold a penalty unless there has
been a "clear abuse of discretion" on the part of the administrative
agency. On the other hand, courts will invalidate a penalty imposed
by an administrative agency if it is arbitrary or capricious,
patently abusive, oppressive or clearly excessive.2/ These standards,
while they are far from precise, at least indicate that Commission
decisions concerning imposition of penalties will not be lightly
overturned by the courts.

In specific cases, courts have elaborated on these principles,
and have explained in somewhat more detail what factors a commission
such as the Fair Political Practices Commission should or should
not, consider in imposing penalties. The Commission should,
first of all, take into account "all relevant facts."2/ The
courts have identified some such relevant facts. These include
the gravity of the violation, the degree of culpability, prior
misconduct, and ability to pay of the violator, the size of the
business which has committed the violation, and the effect of the
violation on the public.4/ The circumstances surrounding the
misbehavior, the degree to which it affects the public service
and the likelihood of its recurrence are relevant.5/ Potential

_________
2/ Collins v. Board of Medical Examiners, 29 Cal. App. 3d
439, 446 (1974); Lake v. Civil Service Commission, 47 Cal. App. 3d
224, 228 (1975); Cadilla v. Board of Medical Examiners, 26 Cal.
App. 3d 961, 966-968 (1972); Blake v. State Personnel Board, 25
Cal. App. 3d 541, 553 (1974).

3/ Blake v. State Personnel Board, 25 Cal. App. 3d 541, 553
(1972).

4/ Hale v. Morgan, 22 Cal. 3d 388, 399 (1978).

5/ Blake v. State Personnel Board, 35 Cal. App. 3d 541, 554
(1972).

Commissioners Houston, Lapan,
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn
March 20, 1980
Page Three


discredit to a government agency is another valid consideration.6/
The Commission can consider the violator's occupation and expertise,
as well as the nature of the offense and circumstances surrounding
its commission.7/ It is relevant whether, and when, a violator
eventually complied with his obligations under the law (if the
compliance came only after discovery or the initiation of proceedings
against the person, it is not as compelling a factor in mitigation
as it would be if compliance had been voluntarily undertaken
prior to discovery).8/

     One factor which has been repeatedly mentioned by the courts
as being relevant is the financial situation of the person being
punished. As one court said, "A relatively small penalty might
suffice for the small operator, while the same penalty would be
paid with little injury to the wealthy one."9/ Similarly, a fine
which might be appropriate for a wealthy person might be unduly
oppressive for someone less wealthy.

     The Commission cannot avoid its responsibility to tailor the
penalty to the particular offense and offender before it. Fairness
does not require that "charges similar in nature must result in
identical penalties." Nor is the Commission "bound to deal with
all cases at all times in the same manner as it had dealt with
some past cases that might seem comparable."10/ On the contrary,
fairness requires that the Commission consider each case individually.
This is illustrated by a case recently decided by the California
Supreme Court, concerning a statute which did not allow for such
individualized consideration of civil penalties but instead required

_______________________

     6/ CF, Lake v. Civil Service Commission, 47 Cal. App. 3d
224, 228 (1975).

     7/ Cadilla v. Board of Medical Examiners, 26 Cal. App. 3d
961, 968 (1972).

     8/ Brown v. Gordon, 240 Cal. App. 2d 659, 667-668 (1966).
Also, Section 81004.5 of the Political Reform Act specifically
states that "Amending an incorrect or incomplete report or statement
may be considered evidence of good faith."

     9/ People v. Superior Court, 35 Cal. App. 3d 710, 713 (1973).

     10/ Marino v. City of Los Angeles, 34 Cal. App. 3d 461, 466
(1973). However, the Commission may adopt a flexible policy as to
penalties Harris v. Alcoholic Beverage Control Appeals Bd., 62
Cal. 2d 589, 595-07 (1965).

Commissioners Houston, Japan,
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn
March 20, 1980
Page Four

a set fine to be imposed for each of a series of repeated viola-
tions of a statute.11/

In that case, the court criticized the statute because "the
fixed penalties are imposed upon potential defends who may vary
greatly in sophistication and financial strength...." It went on
to hold that the Legislature cannot, "in defiance of due process
requirements, compel the exaction of penalties which, in a particu-
lar case, demonstrably overbalance and outweigh reasonable goals
of punishment, regulation, and deterrence." If the legislature
may not fail to take individual circumstances into account in
setting the amount of a fine, neither can an administrative body.

The Commission can also take broad policy considerations into
account in imposing penalties for violation of the Act. It is not
limited in its deliberations to the specific circumstances of the
individual offense and offender before it. The Commission can
properly consider additional factors, such as the need to deter
others from committing future violations of the Act.12/ For example,
in the case of a violation of a type which it believes to be wide-
spread, the Commission may impose a penalty which is intended, at
least in part, to demonstrate that similar violations by others
will not be treated lightly.13/

In general, it can be said that the Commission can properly
consider almost anything having to do with the offender or the
offense, as well as with the need to deter future violations of
the Act, in imposing penalties. In fact, the Commission is more
likely to run into problems by failing to consider relevant
factors, than by considering possibly irrelevant ones. Where

____________

11/ Hale v. Morgan, 22 Cal. 3d 388, 399, 402-403 (1978).

12/ People v. Superior Court, 35 Cal. App. 3d 710, 713 (1973);
People v. Witzerman, 29 Cal. App. 3d 169, 177 (1972).

13/ Collins v. Board of Medical Examiners, 29 Cal. App. 3d
439, 446 (1972); Martin v. Alcoholic Beverage Control Appeals
Board, 52 Cal. 2d 287, 293 (1959).

Commissioners Houston, Lapan,
McAndrews, Metzger and Quinn
March 20, 1980
Page Five


reasonable people might differ as to the propriety of the particular
penalty imposed, the administrative agency's determination will
almost always be upheld by the courts.14/

BM:plh


14/ Lake v. Civil Service Commission, 47 Cal. App. 3d 224,
228 (1975); Cadilla v. Board of Medical Examiners, 26 Cal. App. 3d
961, 968 (1972); Brown v. Gordon, 240 Cal. App. 2d 659, 670 (1966).

sections.question_synthetic: What is the scope of the FPPC Commission's discretion in imposing penalties under Section 83114 of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Commission has broad discretion in imposing penalties, guided by statutory factors (seriousness, intent, good faith, pattern of violations), regulatory criteria, and court-developed principles requiring consideration of all relevant circumstances including gravity, culpability, prior misconduct, financial ability, and deterrence effects. The Commission must tailor penalties to individual cases rather than impose identical penalties for similar violations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum from staff to Commissioners, not a response to an external request. No traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections; structured as legal analysis with extensive case law citations. Document discusses penalty-setting framework and judicial standards for administrative agency discretion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the scope of the FPPC Commission's discretion in imposing penalties under Section 83114 of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The Commission has broad discretion in imposing penalties, guided by statutory factors (seriousness, intent, good faith, pattern of violations), regulatory criteria, and court-developed principles requiring consideration of all relevant circumstances including gravity, culpability, prior misconduct, financial ability, and deterrence effects. The Commission must tailor penalties to individual cases rather than impose identical penalties for similar violations.

embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum to Commissioners analyzing the scope of the Commission's discretion in imposing civil penalties under the Political Reform Act, discussing statutory factors, regulatory criteria, court-developed standards, and relevant considerations for individualized penalty determination.
